title: Tapping for Generalised Anxiety
subtitle: An EFT sequence for the background hum of anxiety that doesn't attach to one specific thing
excerpt: An EFT tapping sequence for generalised anxiety: the kind without a single clear source, the body that stays on alert, the mind that won't settle. Taps through the alarm state and toward a nervous system that can register safety rather than constant low-level threat.
sourceNotes: Written for homemade.education. EFT framework developed by Gary Craig (mid-1990s).

practice.whenToUse: Use when generalised anxiety is running — the background hum that doesn't attach to one specific thing. Can be used daily as part of a nervous system regulation practice.

content.text: An EFT tapping sequence for anxiety without a single identifiable cause. The kind that runs in the background: a body that stays alert, a mind that moves from one worry to the next without landing, a persistent readiness for something that hasn't happened yet. This sequence works on the alarm state, not the thoughts that feed it.
New to tapping? Read
how tapping works
first.
The practice
Karate chop
Tap the karate chop point and say each statement once:
Even though I'm anxious and I can't always explain why, I deeply and completely accept myself.
Even though my body stays on alert even when there is no immediate threat, I accept this is where I am right now.
Even though the anxiety is exhausting and I don't know how to turn it off, I'm willing to give my body a moment to settle.
Tapping round
Tap each point seven times while saying the phrase:
Eyebrow: The hum of anxiety that's always there.
Side of Eye: The body that won't relax even when everything is fine.
Under Eye: Waiting for something to go wrong.
Under Nose: The mind moving from one thing to the next without stopping.
Chin: I can't name it exactly. I just know it's there.
Collarbone: The exhaustion of being ready for a threat that hasn't arrived.
Under Arm: The gap between knowing I'm safe and feeling it.
Top of Head: This anxiety I carry that doesn't have a single address.
Reframe
Eyebrow: The anxiety is my nervous system working hard. It is not my character.
Side of Eye: The body can learn to register safety. That is what this practice is for.
Under Eye: Right now, in this moment, I am safe. My body can take that in.
Under Nose: I can give my mind a moment to land here instead of ahead.
Chin: This anxiety is workable. I've worked with it before.
Collarbone: I can turn the alarm down a little, even when I can't turn it off entirely.
Under Arm: Knowing it's anxiety is itself useful. I can work with what I can name.
Top of Head: I am calmer than I was a minute ago. That counts.
Tap each round three times, then move to the reframe and tap that three times.
Where this practice comes from
Written for homemade.education. The EFT framework is Gary Craig's Emotional Freedom Technique, in use since the mid-1990s.
